draft.introduction: Iamblichus’s De Mysteriis (c. 300 CE) defends theurgy—ritual action that unites the soul with the gods—against Porphyry’s rationalist objections. Book I establishes that the soul’s ascent to the divine requires not philosophy alone but sacred rites transmitted by the gods themselves.

translation.text: It is not thought that links theurgists to the gods. For what would prevent those who practice theoretical philosophy from enjoying the theurgic union with the gods? In truth it is the performance of acts not to be divulged, acts beyond all comprehension, and the power of the unutterable symbols understood by the gods alone, that establish theurgic union.
translation.original_text: οὐχ ἡ ἔννοια συνάπτει τοὺς θεουργοὺς τοῖς θεοῖς

translator_notes.note: Iamblichus’s defense of theurgy marked a decisive turn in Neoplatonism: the soul’s ascent requires not just intellectual contemplation (as Plotinus held) but ritual practice. This theurgic turn influenced all later Neoplatonism and, through Pseudo-Dionysius, shaped Christian liturgical theology.
